# RFC-0002: Benchmark output contract semantics

> **Version:** 0.1.0 | **Status:** normative | **Phase:** stable

---

## 1. Summary

### [RFC-0002:C-SUMMARY] Summary (Informative) <a id="rfc-0002c-summary"></a>

This RFC defines the semantic meaning of benchmark output contracts used by `agent-skill-bench`.

**Scope:** required section ordering, heading-level interpretation, optional title blocks, section-body boundaries, and deterministic contract checks.

**Rationale:** benchmark suites need outputs that are comparable across runs without forcing every suite to invent its own parser semantics.

*Since: v0.1.0*

---

## 2. Specification

### [RFC-0002:C-REQUIRED-SECTIONS] Required section semantics (Normative) <a id="rfc-0002c-required-sections"></a>

A prompt contract MAY declare an ordered list of required section titles for a benchmark mode.
When required sections are declared, the candidate output MUST contain each required section exactly once and in the declared order at the contract's required heading level.
The contract MUST define the heading level at which required sections are interpreted.
Nested headings below the required heading level MUST remain part of the parent section body until the next heading at the same or higher level is encountered.

**Rationale:** benchmark comparability depends on stable section boundaries, especially when candidates add internal subheadings inside an otherwise valid section.

*Since: v0.1.0*

### [RFC-0002:C-TITLE-BLOCK] Optional title block semantics (Normative) <a id="rfc-0002c-title-block"></a>

A prompt contract MAY allow an optional title block before the first required section.
When a title block is allowed, it MUST consist of at most one level-1 heading and MAY include a thematic break immediately after that heading.
A title block MUST NOT include introductory prose or any additional content before the first required section.
When a title block is not allowed, the output MUST begin with the first required section except for leading whitespace.

**Rationale:** benchmark contracts must support natural markdown document structure without allowing uncontrolled preambles that reduce comparability.

*Since: v0.1.0*

### [RFC-0002:C-DETERMINISTIC-CONTRACT-CHECKS] Deterministic contract checks (Normative) <a id="rfc-0002c-deterministic-contract-checks"></a>

When a prompt contract declares required sections, deterministic assessment MUST check required-section presence, uniqueness, ordering, and non-emptiness against the contract semantics.
Deterministic assessment MUST prefer the resolved section body at the contract heading level for section-targeted rules.
If no section with the required title exists at the contract heading level, an implementation MAY fall back to a section with the same title at another heading level for suite-owned section-targeted rules.
Such fallback MUST NOT satisfy required-section presence, ordering, uniqueness, or non-emptiness checks for the contract itself.
Deterministic assessment MUST emit stable failure-mode codes for failed contract or rule checks so benchmark reports can aggregate repeated failures.
The framework MAY enforce additional suite-owned deterministic rules beyond the generic contract checks, but those rules MUST be expressed as suite-owned policy data rather than implicit parser behavior.

**Rationale:** suites need both shared baseline checks and suite-specific deterministic checks, but the resulting failure signals must remain stable enough for regression analysis. The current framework also preserves a limited title-based fallback for suite-owned rules without letting that fallback mask contract failures.

*Since: v0.1.0*

---
